channel.title: The Grant Desk - US, next 90 days
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: US, next 90 days.

item.title: Adrianna Newell Fellowship at Arts&Rec 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Arts&Rec Inc

Location: Stamford, NY (Western Catskills), USA

Award: Live/work residency: use of a 1000 sq ft studio and attached apartment, plus a $1,500 total materials stipend and per diem. Travel (especially international) is not funded. No application fee.

Duration: October 2026 (four weeks to two months, based on project needs; possible extension into November)

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Inaugural Adrianna Newell Fellowship, a live/work studio residency in Stamford, NY (Western Catskills) for October 2026, with possible extension into November. Named for board member Adrianna Newell, it invites queer lens-based artists working in photography and moving image with an active, research-driven or conceptually rich practice, especially work engaging queer perspectives on land, rurality and regional space, or queer identity, memory, intimacy, community and embodiment. Open to artists from everywhere. Fellows receive a 1000 sq ft studio with attached apartment, a $1,500 combined materials stipend and per diem, and introductions to the local creative community; an open-studio presentation, artist talk or public exhibition at the end is required. Travel, especially international, is not funded (residents are encouraged to seek travel support elsewhere). Apply by emailing a single PDF (one-page description of your work and planned project, 10 to 20 work samples with short descriptions, and a current CV) to programs@artsrec.org. Deadline: 31 July 2026; notices by end of August.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: James Castle House: 2027 Artist Residencies (Boise, Idaho)
item.description:
Organisation: James Castle House / City of Boise

Location: Boise, Idaho, USA. Open to applicants legally able to work in the United States.

Award: $4,000 stipend for two-month terms or $1,000 for two-week terms, to assist with living expenses and supplies, plus solo live-in accommodation and studio at the historic James Castle House. Residents donate one selected piece to the City of Boise collection at the end of the term. No application fee.

Duration: Two-month terms (1 Mar - 30 Apr, 1 Jun - 31 Jul, 1 Sep - 31 Oct 2027) or two-week terms (11-25 Jan and 1-15 Dec 2027)

Deadline: 28 Aug 2026

The James Castle House in Boise, Idaho (the preserved home of self-taught artist James Castle) opens applications for its 2027 residency terms from 16 July to 28 August 2026. TWO-MONTH RESIDENCY: an immersive focus on a project or body of work responding to the history and legacy of James Castle or the site; open to artists, scholars, creators and professionals in any field; $4,000 stipend; residents share their practice through public events (workshops, open studios, presentations) and donate a selected piece to the City of Boise collection. Terms: 1 March - 30 April, 1 June - 31 July, or 1 September - 31 October 2027. TWO-WEEK RESIDENCY: time and space for a single site-responsive project; $1,000 stipend; one public event. Terms: 11-25 January or 1-15 December 2027. A rolling PROJECT RESIDENCY also considers proposals case by case (email jamescastlehouse@cityofboise.org). ELIGIBILITY: 18+, legally able to work in the United States, able to pass a background check; emerging and mid-career practitioners in any field, though the property cannot accommodate loud or especially messy processes. Residents live alone on site. Selection by committee on strength of proposal, application, work samples and references. Virtual information session 21 July 2026, 1pm MT. Applicants notified September 2026. DEADLINE: 28 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Wave Hill / AnkhLave Arts Alliance: 2027 Van Lier Fellowship (NYC)
item.description:
Organisation: Wave Hill / AnkhLave Arts Alliance

Location: Wave Hill Public Garden and Cultural Center, the Bronx, New York City, USA. For NYC-based BIPOC visual artists aged 30 or younger.

Award: $10,435 stipend, including an $8,000 honorarium, a transportation stipend for the residency, and professional development opportunities, plus the Winter Workspace residency, a solo exhibition with public programs, mentorship from Wave Hill curatorial staff and arts professionals, and AnkhLave Alumni Network benefits. Two fellowships awarded. No application fee.

Duration: January to December 2027; Winter Workspace residency 23 February - 18 April 2027; solo exhibition 15 May - 4 July 2027

Deadline: 10 Aug 2026

Wave Hill, in partnership with AnkhLave Arts Alliance, invites NYC-based artists who identify as BIPOC to apply for the 2027 Van Lier Fellowship as part of the 9th Annual AnkhLave Garden Project at Wave Hill Public Garden and Cultural Center in the Bronx. The project supports artists who draw on research, scholarship and lived knowledge to present site-responsive work at the intersection of nature, culture and place, engaging with Wave Hill's plant collections, history, design and architecture. The Van Lier Fellowship (funded by the Edward and Sally Van Lier Fund through the New York Community Trust) will be awarded to two NYC-based visual artists aged 30 or younger. FELLOWSHIP: participation in the Winter Workspace residency (23 February - 18 April 2027); a $10,435 stipend including an $8,000 honorarium and transportation stipend; a solo exhibition in the Sunroom or Sun Porch (15 May - 4 July 2027); public engagement through Winter Workspace programming and a Meet the Artist program; mentorship with Wave Hill curatorial staff, working artists and arts professionals; and AnkhLave Alumni Network benefits. ELIGIBILITY: emerging artists 30 or younger (born 1997 or after) from historically underrepresented communities; live in one of the five NYC boroughs; demonstrate financial need; have completed college and not be enrolled in a degree-granting program during the residency; not represented by a commercial gallery; available for both programs; cannot have been a Van Lier Fellow elsewhere or have previously done a Winter Workspace residency or exhibited at Wave Hill or with AnkhLave. Works may not include nudity, depictions of violence or profanity. Artists may apply to only one of the two 2027 AnkhLave Garden Project opportunities. Questions: visualarts@wavehill.org with '2027 Van Lier-Ankhlave' in the subject. DEADLINE: 10 August 2026. Apply via SlideRoom.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Woodward/Newman Award 2027-28 (Constellation Stage & Screen)
item.description:
Organisation: Constellation Stage & Screen

Location: Bloomington, Indiana, USA. Open submissions of unpublished plays; production takes place in Bloomington.

Award: USD 3,000 cash prize (in lieu of royalties) plus a full production in Constellation's Mainstage season; housing and transportation provided. No submission fee.

Duration: Winner announced by 15 May 2027; production in the 2027-28 season. Submissions stay on file and under consideration for two years.

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

The Woodward/Newman Award, started with the support of Joanne Woodward, Newman's Own Foundation and the Newman family, is awarded annually by Constellation Stage & Screen to the best unpublished new play. The winner receives a USD 3,000 cash prize (in lieu of royalties) and a full production in the Mainstage season, with housing and transportation provided. The contest format and submission fees have been eliminated: submissions are reviewed on an ongoing basis and are also considered for all seven production slots in the season, including TYA (theatre for young audiences) shows. Full-length plays should run 75 to 135 minutes; TYA shows over 40 minutes. Plays must be unpublished at submission (independently published is acceptable); limit of two submissions per playwright per year; musicals may include demos. Plays submitted by midnight 31 August 2026 are considered for the 2027-28 season; later submissions roll to 2028-29. All submissions are kept on file for two years.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Railyard Art Project: Call for Temporary Public Art (Santa Fe)
item.description:
Organisation: Railyard Art Project (Railyard Park + Plaza)

Location: Santa Fe, New Mexico, United States (Railyard Park + Plaza, City-owned property under a Conservation Easement).

Award: USD 3,500 honorarium per project (two projects selected), plus marketing support and liability insurance. Funded by Santa Fe's Arts and Culture Commission. No application fee.

Duration: Two projects hosted between October 2026 and June 2027.

Deadline: 05 Aug 2026

The Railyard Art Project (RAP) facilitates temporary public art installations by established and emerging artists and arts collectives in Santa Fe's Railyard Park + Plaza. RAP is accepting proposals for temporary public art installations and creative projects; thanks to funding from Santa Fe's Arts and Culture Commission, two projects will be hosted between October 2026 and June 2027, each receiving a USD 3,500 honorarium plus marketing support and liability insurance. THEMES: the RAP Committee encourages collaboration across disciplines (art, science, history) and welcomes thoughtful, challenging, playful, and edifying art in the context of social and cultural expression and environmental sustainability. Proposed projects must physically withstand outdoor public display and engage the community by responding to the natural and social landscape of the park. SELECTION CRITERIA: overall artistic merit; context and familiarity with the proposed site; practical viability; and potential for community engagement and cultural/educational enrichment. Artists should select an eligible area of the park's open space and describe the project in detail; photos or renderings may be uploaded with the application (a map of Railyard Art Locations is linked from the program page). The RAP Committee, made up of community members and Park + Plaza stakeholders, selects and schedules the exhibitions; a formal in-person presentation may be requested depending on complexity. CONDITIONS: the park operates under a Conservation Easement, so installations cannot be located on planted areas, no staking of any kind is permitted (including during install/de-install), and nothing may be hung from trees. Artists lend artwork at their own risk (no protection against vandalism or weather damage, and artists are responsible for repairs). Installs and de-installs must be coordinated in advance with the RAP Committee or Railyard Park Conservancy staff, with a Committee or staff member on site throughout. Application deadline 5 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Target Margin Theater: TMT Institute Fellowship 2026-27
item.description:
Organisation: Target Margin Theater

Location: Brooklyn, New York, United States (Sunset Park). Fellows must be available in person for intensives, monthly breakfasts, and excursions.

Award: USD 2,000 stipend, plus space, material resources, and collaborators to support each Fellow's experiments. No application fee.

Duration: 2026-27 season: three intensive sessions in Brooklyn (5-8 November 2026, 8-10 January 2027, 19-21 March 2027), monthly breakfasts October 2026 through May 2027, and an Open Studio weekend in June 2027.

Deadline: 22 Jul 2026

Target Margin Theater invites applications for its next cohort of TMT Institute Fellows for the 2026/2027 season. The Institute is a cohort of artists who seek to radically disrupt and re-examine their existing practice without any goal or expected result: pure experimentation with no product-oriented end goal. Fellows pursue no project, no developmental stage, no deliverable; misfires, dead ends, and bad ideas are considered a fruitful part of the work. SUPPORT: TMT provides space, material resources, collaborators, and a USD 2,000 stipend. STRUCTURE: three short intensive sessions at Target Margin Theater in Brooklyn (5-8 November 2026, 8-10 January 2027, and 19-21 March 2027, all day with some evening hours), monthly breakfasts at Junior's in Brooklyn (20 October, 30 November, 4 January, 1 February, 8 March, 5 April, 3 May), and occasional excursions. After the second intensive, each Fellow commits to an exploration pursued outside group gatherings, supported by TMT resources. The season ends with an Open Studio weekend in June 2027 to share explorations with the community; this is not a performance and should not be an audience-attended, time-bounded show. WHO CAN APPLY: artists from any discipline within or outside theater, as well as production, technical, and administrative workers in the arts. Applicants must have an established, existing body of work and practice they are committed to changing; do not apply with a project. Recent graduates of training programs are unlikely to be appropriate candidates; no formal education level or degree is required. SELECTION: TMT staff and artistic community review applications and invite a handful of candidates to interview; all applicants are notified by October. Deadline: Wednesday 22 July 2026, 11:59 pm EST.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Alabama Historical Commission: Clotilda Memorial Public Art Commission
item.description:
Organisation: Alabama Historical Commission (with Forecast Public Art)

Location: Africatown, Mobile, Alabama, USA; open to artists internationally.

Award: USD 2,000,000 total project budget for a paid public art commission. No application fee.

Duration: Permanent public art commission

Deadline: 09 Aug 2026

The Alabama Historical Commission, working with public-art consultancy Forecast Public Art, seeks proposals for a major memorial artwork honoring the survivors of the Clotilda, the last known ship to illegally traffic enslaved Africans to the United States (1860). The memorial will convey the survivors' lives and legacies and be developed through collaborative community engagement with historic Africatown, the community founded by Clotilda survivors, and the broader diaspora. ELIGIBILITY: open to artists of all disciplines, including time-based and media artists; individuals and teams; open internationally. BUDGET: USD 2,000,000 total project budget. DEADLINE: 9 August 2026, 11:59pm CST. Applications are handled through the CODAworx RFQ platform; find the Clotilda Memorial listing in the CODAworx opportunities index. Verify the listing details on CODAworx before applying.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AGI Governance Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: Johns Hopkins University, School of Government and Policy (SOGP)

Location: Hopkins Bloomberg Center, Washington, D.C. (in-person only)

Award: Fully funded: travel, accommodation, and meals covered.

Duration: Three-week in-person intensive, 8-25 September 2026

Deadline: 30 Jul 2026

The AGI Governance Fellowship is the inaugural three-week intensive run by Johns Hopkins University's School of Government and Policy (SOGP) to develop the next generation of leaders in AI governance. COVERS: frontier AI legislation at state and federal levels; building societal resilience amid rapid capability growth; democratic practices in AGI alignment; and designing new governing institutions for the AGI era. WHO SHOULD APPLY: early-career researchers and practitioners (within roughly eight years of undergrad) with solid foundations in AI governance, including congressional staffers, AGI company employees, recent CS or policy PhDs, and nonprofit/civil society professionals; a postgraduate degree or equivalent experience is required. This is a capstone program for rising leaders, not an intro course. FUNDING: travel, accommodation, and meals are fully covered. DATES: 8-25 September 2026 at the Hopkins Bloomberg Center in Washington, D.C. (in-person only). DEADLINE: apply by 15 July 2026 for full consideration; rolling applications accepted. APPLY: https://sogp.jh.edu/agi-governance-fellowship/

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Horizon Institute for Public Service: AI Rapid Response Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: Horizon Institute for Public Service

Location: Washington, DC area, United States (in-person; full-time placements in U.S. executive branch offices).

Award: Fellow track: USD 170,000 to 200,000+, plus an additional stipend for benefits. Senior Fellow track: from USD 250,000+, plus a benefits stipend, commensurate with the seniority of the placement.

Duration: One year full-time, with the opportunity for renewal.

Deadline: 22 Jul 2026

The AI Rapid Response Fellowship is a focused effort by the Horizon Institute for Public Service to close the AI security talent gap in the U.S. federal government. Horizon is recruiting a specialized cohort of exceptional technical and policy talent to work full-time in executive branch offices on AI security-related issues, with placements beginning as soon as possible. TRACKS: the Fellow track is for candidates with technical or policy expertise relevant to AI security, typically 2-6+ years of full-time relevant experience; the Senior Fellow track is for candidates with substantial prior experience, typically 10-15+ years in relevant technical, policy, or leadership roles. FOCUS: AI governance, AI security, policy, and public service. COMPENSATION: Fellow track USD 170,000-200,000+ plus a benefits stipend; Senior Fellow track from USD 250,000+ plus a benefits stipend, commensurate with the seniority of the placement. DURATION: one year full-time, with the opportunity for renewal, based in the Washington, DC area. ELIGIBILITY: U.S. citizenship is required. An existing security clearance, or eligibility to obtain one, is helpful but not required. DEADLINE: applications are due July 22, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: America On Tech: Tech Flex Leaders Program
item.description:
Organisation: America On Tech

Location: United States. Remote/hybrid program.

Award: USD 500 stipend on completion, plus an America On Tech certificate. No application fee.

Duration: Program cohort (dates per program).

Deadline: 17 Aug 2026

America On Tech's Tech Flex Leaders Program is open to young people interested in tech who are curious, resourceful, and ready to build something new. No prior tech experience is required. Participants earn an America On Tech certificate and receive a USD 500 stipend for completing the program. WHO IT'S FOR: young people interested in tech; no prior tech experience required. AWARD: USD 500 stipend plus certificate. DEADLINE: 5 July 2026 priority, open until 17 August 2026. TO APPLY: submit the application via the America On Tech program page.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Queer|Art Design Grant 2026 (Inaugural)
item.description:
Organisation: Queer|Art (in partnership with UGG)

Location: United States; open to US-based emerging queer/LGBTQ+ designers.

Award: Two USD 10,000 grants (described as the largest cash grant for LGBTQ+ designers in the US). No application fee.

Duration: One-off grant; winners announced at Queer|Art's Annual Party, Fall 2026

Deadline: 14 Aug 2026

The Queer|Art Design Grant is a new initiative from Queer|Art supporting projects that explore queer histories, legacies, and futures while providing visibility and opportunity for emerging LGBTQ+ design talent. AWARDS: two USD 10,000 grants to emerging queer creatives across the design industry, described as the largest cash grant for LGBTQ+ designers in the country. ELIGIBILITY: US-based designers working across a variety of disciplines, including graphic design, architecture and landscape architecture, industrial and product design, fashion, textile and wearables, digital design, service design, furniture and home decor, materials research, interior design, and more. In its inaugural year the grant is judged by a panel of designers, curators, architects, and cultural leaders, including Bex McCharen, Salome Asega, Cristina Grajales, Jaffer Kolb, and Paul Soulellis. The program is the first major initiative under a new partnership between UGG and Queer|Art; Queer|Art board member and creative strategist Fran Tirado serves as Community Curator. Winners will be announced at Queer|Art's Annual Party in Fall 2026. DEADLINE: 14 August 2026. Apply via https://www.queer-art.org/design-grant.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Wassaic Project 2027 Summer Exhibition Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: The Wassaic Project

Location: Wassaic, New York, United States (in and around historic Wassaic and Maxon Mills).

Award: A modest honorarium for all accepted artists (part of the organization's effort toward W.A.G.E. certification). Artists creating site-specific installations are also eligible for an Exhibitions Fellowship and a no-fee residency of 1-4 weeks in April or May 2027.

Duration: The 2027 Summer Exhibition runs free and open to the public every weekend, 1 May to 12 September 2027. Optional fellowship residency of 1-4 weeks in April or May 2027.

Deadline: 10 Aug 2026

The Wassaic Project is holding its annual Open Call for the 2027 Summer Exhibition, for artists of all mediums, including 2D work, sculpture, video, new media, site-specific installation, performance, text, poems, essays, and publication-specific work. Selected work is showcased alongside a diverse range of pieces and performances in and around historic Wassaic and Maxon Mills; the 2027 Summer Exhibition will be free and open to the public every weekend from 1 May to 12 September 2027. In an effort toward W.A.G.E. certification, all accepted artists receive a modest honorarium to participate. Artists interested in creating a site-specific installation are also eligible for an Exhibitions Fellowship to help realize their work; Fellows are offered a no-fee residency for 1-4 weeks in April or May 2027 (artists interested in making site-specific work should still apply regardless of whether they are interested in or able to be in residence). The Wassaic Project cultivates and supports community for emerging and professional contemporary artists, writers, and other creatives, with a residency program housed in historic landmark buildings that hosts 10 artists each month in the heart of a rural community. APPLICATION: requires application forms, a CV, and an artwork/portfolio; submitted via SlideRoom. Application fee: USD 25 (no participation fee or additional fees). Deadline 10 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Genesis Mission AI Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Horizon Institute for Public Service (with Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science)

Location: United States. Fellows are forward deployed to US Department of Energy national labs (initial partners: Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory). Remote or in-person; on-site travel at least once a quarter. Must be eligible to work in the US (no visa sponsorship; US green card holders eligible).

Award: Prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000.

Duration: 9-12 months, starting fall 2026.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Horizon Institute for Public Service, Renaissance Philanthropy, SeedAI, and Fulcrum Science are partnering to launch a fellowship that embeds technical AI talent across the US Department of Energy national labs to advance the goals of the Genesis Mission, DOE's flagship science and technology initiative launched in late 2025. The Genesis Mission identifies 26 national science and technology challenges critical to US leadership and envisions a national platform connecting supercomputers, experimental facilities, AI systems, and unique datasets, with DOE's 17 national labs playing a key role. Genesis Mission AI Fellows are forward deployed to DOE national labs to bring frontier AI expertise to high-priority scientific challenges. As core members of the technical staff on a Genesis Mission project, fellows work alongside national lab scientists and partners and apply AI to national priorities, from building cutting-edge models of plasma dynamics in pursuit of controlled fusion to advancing state-of-the-art models for foundational materials discovery. Initial national lab partners include Idaho National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, and the Princeton Plasma Physics Laboratory; additional partners may be announced later. COMPENSATION/DURATION: 9-12 months starting fall 2026, with a prorated total annual compensation of USD 200,000; fellows are expected to travel to their national lab site at least once a quarter but may work on-site if they prefer. ELIGIBILITY: competitive applicants should have at least 2 years of industry experience building AI models or agents (those with industry experience building AI applications or data infrastructure will also be considered); scientific domain expertise is not required, but any placement will involve some training and study in a specific scientific discipline or energy topic, supported by expert colleagues at the host institution. All fellows must be eligible to work in the United States for the duration of the placement; US employment visas cannot be sponsored, but US permanent residents (green card holders) are eligible. APPLICATION: initial form requires a CV or resume (max 2 pages) and short-answer questions on motivations and experiences (max 200 words); successful applicants complete interviews through partner organizations and national labs, and selected fellows are matched to partner labs by scientific domain and availability. The review uses evidence-based best practices including blind grading of application materials. TIMELINE: application deadline 31 July 2026; final fellow selection August-September 2026; expected start fall 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: NEIU Art + Design Studio Art Residency 2026-2027
item.description:
Organisation: Northeastern Illinois University (NEIU), Art + Design Department

Location: Salme Harju Steinberg Fine Arts Center, Northeastern Illinois University, Chicago, Illinois, United States. In-person; semester-long access to NEIU facilities.

Award: USD 800 honorarium, plus a semester of full access to NEIU Art + Design facilities specific to the project (Art + Tech, Ceramics, Drawing, Painting, Photography, Printmaking, Sculpture); supplies can be requested with guidance and approval from Studio Area personnel. Visiting Artists may also exhibit in a group exhibition in the NEIU Fine Arts Center Gallery.

Duration: One semester. Residency Session 1: 25 January - 14 May; Session 2: 30 August - 11 December.

Deadline: 09 Aug 2026

The NEIU Art + Design Studio Art Residency strives to create visible connections between Northeastern Illinois University's diverse population of art and design students and working artists who embody these identities, providing insights and inspiration through a dynamic exchange of ideas and techniques. Visiting Artists should demonstrate a commitment to their artistic practice and an interest in engaging with NEIU's students and creative community. Each Visiting Artist is awarded an honorarium and has full access for a semester to the NEIU Art + Design facilities specific to their project; over the residency they showcase their work in a public academic forum, lead a hands-on workshop, and participate in a Portfolio Review Day for NEIU's Art + Design students. FACILITIES (in the Salme Harju Steinberg Fine Arts Center) include a Design Studio with the largest collection of Riso drums at any Chicago university, a Fab Lab (FDM and resin 3D printers, large-format CNC router, vinyl cutters, laser cutters), a Ceramic Studio (throwing wheels, glaze room, electric and gas kilns, raku area), a Letterpress Studio (two Vandercook presses, metal and wood type), a Sculpture Studio (wood shop, clay modeling and spray painting areas), a Photography Studio (analog and digital, plus a lighting studio), and fully equipped Painting, Drawing, and Printmaking studios. BENEFITS: USD 800 honorarium; facility access based on project focus (Art + Tech, Ceramics, Drawing, Painting, Photography, Printmaking, Sculpture), with supplies requestable subject to approval; and the opportunity to exhibit in a group exhibition in the NEIU Fine Arts Center Gallery. PARTICIPATION: lead a 2-hour hands-on workshop for 15-20 students on a topic relevant to the project; give an hour-long artist talk with Q&A; and serve as a guest panelist for a day-long portfolio review. KEY DATES: open call 2 June - 9 August; emailed acceptances in October; Residency Session 1 runs 25 January - 14 May and Session 2 runs 30 August - 11 December. Apply via the JotForm application. Deadline 9 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: 100 Voices of Florida Film Grant
item.description:
Organisation: 100 Voices of Florida

Location: Florida, United States

Award: Up to USD 5,000 (unrestricted) USD 25 application fee with a Florida connection, USD 35 without

Duration: One-time grant

Deadline: 20 Sep 2026

100 Voices of Florida is a grant for filmmakers and screenwriters that awards up to USD 5,000 in unrestricted funds, which winners can use for finishing funds, festival submission fees, cast and crew reimbursements, production, or anything else. Applicants submit either a short film (under twenty minutes) or a script (twenty pages or less); films in other languages must include accurate English subtitles, and scripts must be in English. The grant is open to writers, directors, and producers who are at least 18 years old, and creative teams may submit, though only one entrant receives the award. A panel of judges with professional filmmaking experience evaluates entries, and the winner also receives a meeting with an industry professional. The deadline for the current cycle is September 20, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Dedalus Foundation Senior Fellowship 2027
item.description:
Organisation: The Dedalus Foundation

Location: United States (remote, project-based)

Award: Stipend up to USD 30,000

Duration: Up to one year

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Dedalus Foundation, established by artist Robert Motherwell, awards a Senior Fellowship supporting critical and historical studies related to painting, sculpture, and allied arts of the twentieth century. The fellowship is open to art historians, critics, curators, and other writers and scholars who have demonstrated their abilities through previous accomplishments. Awards provide a stipend of up to USD 30,000 for projects lasting up to one year, with the amount varying by project needs. Applicants must be U.S. citizens and may not be currently pursuing academic degrees. Applications are submitted online and reference letters through a separate form. A distinguished scholar jury selects recipients, with awards announced by mid-December.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Jentel Artist Residency
item.description:
Organisation: Jentel Foundation

Location: Banner, Wyoming, USA

Award: USD 100 weekly stipend (about USD 300 total over three weeks), plus private bedroom, separate studio space, and shared living and kitchen facilities provided at no cost USD 30 application fee

Duration: Approximately 3 weeks (arrive on the 15th, depart the morning of the 7th of the following month)

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Jentel Artist Residency offers approximately three-week residencies in the foothills of the Bighorn Mountains near Banner, Wyoming, giving artists, writers, and composers dedicated time for creative work. Sessions run from mid-January through early December, hosting six residents at a time. It is open to visual artists in any medium, writers of fiction, creative nonfiction, or poetry, and composers across musical genres. Residents receive a private bedroom, a separate studio, shared living and kitchen space, and a USD 100 weekly stipend to help cover food and personal expenses. Applicants must be at least 25 years old and be U.S. citizens or foreign nationals whose primary residence is in the U.S. during the application year, and may not be enrolled full-time in a degree program. There is a USD 30 non-refundable application fee, with deadlines of September 15 for Winter and Spring sessions and January 15 for Summer and Fall sessions.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: The Center for Fiction / Susan Kamil Emerging Writer Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: The Center for Fiction

Location: New York City, USA

Award: USD 5,000 grant plus writers studio space, mentorship, agent introductions, and community programming

Duration: One year (the fellowship year)

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Center for Fiction / Susan Kamil Emerging Writer Fellowship annually supports nine early-career, New York City-based fiction writers at a pivotal point in their careers. Each fellow receives a 5,000 USD grant, dedicated space and time to write in the Writers Studio, personalized editorial mentorship, opportunities to meet literary agents, and community programming. Applicants must be current residents of one of the five boroughs and must remain in New York City for the entire fellowship year. An emerging writer is defined as someone who has not yet had a novel or short story collection published by a major or independent publisher and who is not currently under contract for a work of fiction; students enrolled in a degree-granting program during the fellowship year are not eligible. The 2027 fellowship application window opens July 1, 2026 and closes July 31, 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award 2027-2028 (Aix-Marseille, US Faculty)
item.description:
Organisation: Iméra – Aix Marseille Université, in partnership with the Fulbright Commission

Location: Aix-Marseille, France (campus residency)

Award: €2,400 (~USD 2,600) monthly stipend; fully covered accommodation (valued USD 788–1,966/month); €1,200 (~USD 1,300) travel allowance. No application fee.

Duration: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible start/end dates).

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award supports US faculty to conduct research at the Iméra Institute for Advanced Study at Aix Marseille Université. ELIGIBILITY: mid-career, senior, or community college faculty affiliated with a US institution of higher education; US citizens residing outside France (permanent residents of France are ineligible); any discipline relating to Mediterranean studies; English sufficient (French desirable). 1 or 2 grants awarded. PROVIDED: €2,400/month stipend; fully equipped apartment with linens; office in co-working space; visa fees waived; academic event organisation expenses covered; school placement assistance for dependents. DURATION: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible). DEADLINE: 15 September 2026. Apply via the Fulbright Scholar Program at https://fulbrightscholars.org/award/fulbright-imera-mediterranean-studies-award-aix-marseille-university.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: MAK Center: Artists and Architects in Residence 2027/2028 (MAK Schindler Scholarship, Los Angeles)
item.description:
Organisation: MAK Center for Art and Architecture, Los Angeles

Location: Los Angeles, California, United States (Mackey Apartments). Residents must be present in L.A. for the full six-month term.

Award: Six-month MAK Schindler Scholarship residency with accommodation provided at the Mackey Apartments. The open call does not state a stipend, travel or honorarium figure; confirm any additional financial support directly with the MAK Center. No application fee stated.

Duration: Six months. Residency terms run October to March or April to September; 2027/2028 cycle.

Deadline: 12 Aug 2026

The MAK Center for Art and Architecture awards Artists and Architects in Residence scholarships (the MAK Schindler Scholarship) to independent projects exploring the relation between art and architecture. Projects are chosen for their ability to critically examine current trends in art, architecture and society; to make a compelling connection with Los Angeles as the place of implementing or continuing the applicant's own work; and to demonstrate a conceptual and experimental approach. Emphasis is placed on the L.A. location, and residents' projects often reflect, incorporate and address specific facets of the city and its social fabric. Recipients are encouraged to take part in the MAK Center's activities and to attend meetings, workshops and project discussions with other artists and students. ELIGIBILITY: freelance artists; fine arts or architecture students at a university who have completed the '2. Studienabschnitt'; or architects or artists who have recently graduated. Teams of up to three are eligible if all members practice together as an artist group or collective of architects, are all present in L.A. for the six months, and name a contact responsible for the team. HOUSING: the Mackey Apartments are made available to scholarship holders only; family members, relatives and friends may be accommodated for a maximum of two weeks during the term, permitted in advance by Beth Stryker, Director of the MAK Center. Eligibility is excluded if the applicant has been or will be granted another scholarship in the USA immediately before or after the MAK scholarship, or if the data form is incomplete or the required portfolio is missing. Residency terms run October to March and April to September. Deadline 12 August 2026. Apply: https://www.makcenter.org/apply-mackey

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Harvard Radcliffe Institute: Fellowship Program 2027-2028
item.description:
Organisation: Harvard Radcliffe Institute

Location: Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA; fellows must relocate to and reside in the Cambridge/Boston area for the full fellowship (September through May).

Award: USD 78,000 stipend plus an additional USD 5,000 for project expenses. Relocation, housing, and childcare funds may also be provided. No application fee.

Duration: One academic year, September 2027 through May 2028 (residential)

Deadline: 10 Sep 2026

The Harvard Radcliffe Institute Fellowship offers scientists, writers, scholars, public intellectuals, and artists a year to pursue ambitious projects in a vibrant interdisciplinary setting amid the resources of Harvard University. The Institute welcomes innovative work that confronts pressing social, scientific, and policy issues and seeks to engage audiences beyond academia. Reflecting Radcliffe's history, it welcomes (but does not limit eligibility to) proposals focused on women, gender, and society, or that draw on the Schlesinger Library's collections. It also invites proposals relevant to the Institute's 2024-2029 focus area, academic freedom and connecting across difference (intellectual virtues, free and open inquiry, diversity of thought, political polarization, peace and conflict, inequality, religious pluralism, and related higher-education policy issues), including work that constructively challenges disciplinary orthodoxies or advances transformative perspectives. SUPPORT: USD 78,000 stipend plus USD 5,000 for project expenses, with relocation, housing, and childcare funds available; fellows must reside in the Cambridge/Boston area from September through May. ELIGIBILITY: open to individuals across career stages; tenure is not required and applicants need not be academics. This is NOT a postdoctoral fellowship; those currently enrolled in a degree program are ineligible, as are former Harvard Radcliffe fellows (1999-present). Applicants must meet discipline-specific criteria. APPLY: register on the online portal and select an area (Humanities and Social Sciences; Creative Arts; Nonfiction and Journalism; or Science, Engineering, and Mathematics). Materials: application form, CV, 1,400-word project proposal (with bibliography when appropriate), a writing or work sample, and three references. DEADLINES: humanities, social sciences, creative arts, and nonfiction and journalism by 10 September 2026, 5pm ET; science, engineering, and mathematics by 1 October 2026, 5pm ET.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Lighthouse Works: Fellowship Program 2027 (Fishers Island)
item.description:
Organisation: Lighthouse Works

Location: Fishers Island, New York, USA (in-person residency)

Award: Six-week fellowship providing housing, food, private studio space, and USD 1,750 in financial support. No application fee mentioned.

Duration: 6 weeks; fellowships occur year-round across five sessions

Deadline: 15 Oct 2026

Lighthouse Works' Fellowship Program supports a diverse range of cultural producers working at the vanguard of their creative fields. Fellowships are six weeks in length, occur year-round, and provide fellows with housing, food, studio space, and USD 1,750 in financial support. Fellows enjoy a private bedroom and share a kitchen, bathrooms, and living space in a 3-story Victorian house; all dietary needs are accommodated, and on most nights Lighthouse Works staff cook for and eat dinner with the fellows. Studios are located about 1.5 miles (a 30-minute walk) from the fellowship house, are private and flooded with light, and face the ocean adjacent to Silver Eel Cove where the island ferry arrives. Lighthouse Works also maintains a wood and metal fabrication shop and a kiln. While in residence, a fellow's primary obligation is to pursue their own work, though every fellow participates in two events, an Artist Talk and Open Studio, that bookend the fellowship; the program's intimate size allows for conversation, critique, and collaboration. Artistic excellence is the primary criterion for acceptance. Artists at any stage of their career are encouraged to apply through the online Slideroom system. SELECTION: staff review applications for completeness, a jury of experts in each artist's field reviews complete applications and identifies finalists, interviews are scheduled in early January, and applicants are notified in mid-January. The program is supported in part by a grant from the Robert Rauschenberg Foundation. APPLICATION CALL: Lighthouse Works accepts applications each year from September to October (the 2026 portal was open 15 September to 15 October 2025). The deadline shown here is the anticipated close of the next annual call for the 2027 cycle; confirm exact dates on the Lighthouse Works site when the portal reopens. Apply at http://thelighthouseworks.slideroom.com.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: CALI Futures 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Center for Cultural Innovation (CCI)

Location: California, USA. Applicants must reside full-time in California

Award: USD 5,000 project-restricted grants, awarded competitively. USD 100,000 total available each year across the 2026-2028 funding cycle. No application fee.

Duration: Grant support period December 2026 to December 2027

Deadline: 28 Aug 2026

CALI Futures supports artists and cultural workers across California, individually and in teams, who are meaningfully contributing to alternative efforts outside of conventional nonprofit and for-profit arts and culture systems. The Fund encourages work focused on income, ownership, and care, and uplifts the role of artists and cultural workers in shaping and sustaining alternative efforts that provide greater financial stability, strengthen creative ownership, and deepen mutual support across the broader arts ecosystem. Project-restricted grants of USD 5,000 are awarded through a competitive process. Competitive applicants present (a) a clear project request; (b) proof of an active, current artistic or cultural practice; (c) a description of their contribution to an alternative effort that improves financial sustainability, creative ownership, or mutual support and addresses challenges not sufficiently solved by conventional nonprofit or for-profit sectors; and (d) framing that ambitiously describes the larger implications of this work for transforming artists' lives and future cultural possibilities. The Fund supports individuals and teams at every stage, from initial ideas to research, implementation, experimentation, and reflection. ELIGIBILITY: applicants must be individual artists or cultural workers (cultural producers, culture bearers, creatives, cultural practitioners); must reside full-time in California; must be contributing to an alternative effort improving financial sustainability, creative ownership, or mutualistic social support within the arts and culture sector; and that contribution must have occurred or begun on or after 1 January 2020. Teams are eligible, but all team members must meet the criteria and only one application is accepted per team (one member applies and is responsible for grant requirements). INELIGIBLE: individuals without an active practice; organizations seeking operating or program support; arts administrators; individuals living or working outside California; past CALI Catalyst grantees describing the same project funded in 2021-2025; incomplete applications; and those with a conflict of interest with CCI or Hewlett Foundation board, staff, or directors. The Fund is administered by the Center for Cultural Innovation (CCI) with funding from the William and Flora Hewlett Foundation. TIMELINE: applications open 9 June 2026 at 9:00 a.m. PT; deadline 28 August 2026 at 11:59 a.m. PT; review September to November 2026; notifications by 17 November 2026. Submitted via Submittable. Questions: grants@cciarts.org (include CALI FUTURES in the subject line) or 415.288.0530.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: ZwillGen Fellowship Program 2027-2028 (Technology Law)
item.description:
Organisation: ZwillGen

Location: Washington, D.C., USA (must work out of the DC office)

Award: Salary USD 115,000 to USD 125,000, with a reduced annual billable target (1,250 hours) to create space for writing, professional development, and policy engagement. No application fee.

Duration: 12-month position beginning Fall 2027

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

The ZwillGen Fellowship is a 12-month position for recent law-school graduates and early-career attorneys interested in working at the intersection of law, technology, and public policy. Fellows work alongside attorneys on matters involving privacy, cybersecurity, artificial intelligence, online platforms, surveillance, digital advertising, fintech, online safety, and technology regulation, contributing to client counseling, investigations, litigation, and emerging regulatory matters, as well as research, writing, and speaking. The program includes a reduced billable target (1,250 hours) to make room for thought leadership and engagement with the technology policy, privacy, and civil liberties community. ELIGIBILITY: must have graduated from law school before the program starts; must have taken or plan to take a bar exam near the start of the Fellowship; must be able to work out of the DC office and be eligible to apply for admission to the DC Bar. Candidates primarily interested in traditional IP practice may find it less aligned. A small number of applicants may also be considered for the Hannah Schaller Memorial Skylark Fellowship (privacy-law focus, with a reduced 1,100-hour billable target for additional community engagement). Following the Fellowship, fellows may be invited into a full-time attorney role or supported in pursuing other technology law and policy opportunities. APPLY with a resume, law-school transcript, writing sample, and cover letter by 31 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Artadia Awards 2026: Boston
item.description:
Organisation: Artadia

Location: Boston, Massachusetts, United States

Award: Three Awardees each receive a US$15,000 unrestricted award; Finalists receive an honorarium. Plus Artadia Network access and Artist Registry webpage. No application fee.

Duration: One-off award; two-round jury

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

Artadia's open call for Boston, providing unrestricted $15,000 awards to three contemporary visual artists. Finalists not chosen as Awardees receive an honorarium. Two-round jury (curator review + 45-minute virtual studio visits). ELIGIBILITY: living and working in Boston's eligible counties for at least 2 consecutive years prior to deadline; contemporary visual-arts practice; not currently enrolled in an art-related degree programme; no prior Artadia award of $10,000 or more. Open call 15 August - 15 September 2026. Apply via Submittable only.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Artadia Awards 2026: Atlanta
item.description:
Organisation: Artadia

Location: Atlanta metropolitan area, Georgia, United States

Award: Three Awardees each receive a US$15,000 unrestricted award; Finalists receive an honorarium. Plus Artadia Network access and Artist Registry webpage. No application fee.

Duration: One-off award; two-round jury

Deadline: 01 Oct 2026

Artadia's open call for Atlanta, providing unrestricted $15,000 awards to three contemporary visual artists. Finalists receive an honorarium. ELIGIBLE COUNTIES: Barrow, Bartow, Butts, Carroll, Cherokee, Clayton, Cobb, Coweta, DeKalb, Douglas, Fayette, Forsyth, Fulton, Gwinnett, Hall, Henry, Morgan, Newton, Paulding, Pickens, Rockdale, Spalding, and Walton. Must reside in an eligible county for 2+ consecutive years prior to deadline, contemporary visual-arts practice, not a student, no prior Artadia award of $10,000+. Open call 1 September - 1 October 2026. Apply via Submittable only.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Lanesboro Arts BIPOC Artist Residency 2026-2027
item.description:
Organisation: Lanesboro Arts

Location: Lanesboro, Minnesota, United States

Award: Weekly stipend of $1,250 for food, mileage and other costs while in residence (half paid two weeks before residency, half on the final day). Additional budget up to $500 per residency for art supplies if a creative project is pursued. Free private housing (St. Mane Theatre apartment or Art Loft above the gallery) plus access to studio space, kitchen, laundry, Wi-Fi, and an optional Lanesboro Liaison for community/outdoor-recreation connections. No application fee.

Duration: 1 or 2 weeks; residencies take place between September 2026 and April 2027; 8 total weeks distributed across 6-12 residents

Deadline: 10 Aug 2026

Residency for Minnesota-based BIPOC artists (Native American/American Indian/Native Alaskan, Asian, Black/African American, Hispanic/Latino/Chicano/Latinx, Middle Eastern/North African, Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander, SWANA) and their families, run by Lanesboro Arts in rural southeastern MN. NO OUTPUT OR PRODUCT REQUIRED: the residency provides whatever the artist needs (restoration, family getaway, connection to nature, access to rural community, artistic practice) within the context of the community; only one community share-back event is required (workshop, school visit, small gathering, or blog post; arranged with Lanesboro Arts staff after selection). HOUSING: private bedroom + bath in either the St. Mane Theatre Artist Residency Center (sleeps 1-4) or the Art Loft above the Gallery (sleeps 1-5); kitchenette/full kitchen, studio space, laundry, Wi-Fi; neither space is currently ADA-accessible. COHORT OPTION: solo, OR apply with a specific BIPOC artist collaborator, OR apply open to being matched with another BIPOC resident at the same time (both receive the stipend; lodging split between St. Mane and the Art Loft). Spouses/partners only get a stipend if they themselves are applying as artists in the cohort model. SELECTION: lottery process (no traditional artistic-merit gatekeeping); 6-12 residents chosen depending on session lengths; lottery is recorded and shared with applicants for transparency. ELIGIBILITY: BIPOC artists currently based in Minnesota. APPLY via the Lanesboro Arts website. TIMELINE: initial deadline 11:59pm Monday 10 August 2026; lottery the week of 10 August 2026; rolling applications thereafter if slots remain, until 1 April 2027 or until full. Lanesboro Liaison (staff or vetted community member) optionally available for tours, grocery delivery, and outdoor recreation (biking, kayaking, hiking, state parks, etc.).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Rowland Fellowship at Harvard (incoming 2027 cohort)
item.description:
Organisation: The Rowland Institute at Harvard University

Location: Cambridge, Massachusetts, USA (Harvard main campus)

Award: Salary from $89,999 per year with full Harvard benefits; yearly operating budget from $225,000 for lab supplies, travel and hiring personnel (postdoctoral fellows, postbacs, undergraduates); generous start-up funding for capital equipment based on the research programme; dedicated laboratory space plus ancillary spaces (e.g. tissue culture); full principal investigator rights; staff scientist and engineer support for designing and fabricating experimental setups; shared research equipment across Harvard (Center for Nanoscale Systems, Bauer Life Science Core Facility); mentoring on lab culture, scientific writing, budgeting and leadership; and access to Harvard's Core for Mentorship Excellence. No application fee.

Duration: Up to five years, with flexible start (incoming 2027 fellows start July to December 2027)

Deadline: 01 Aug 2026

Rowland Fellowship at Harvard for outstanding early-career experimentalists in any field of science or engineering, providing the opportunity to establish an independent research programme at the Rowland Institute in Cambridge, Massachusetts. Founded by Edwin Land in 1980 to foster high-risk creative research and joined to Harvard in 2002, the Institute particularly supports scholars with potential to establish ground-breaking research programmes that bridge traditional disciplinary boundaries. ELIGIBILITY: applicants should currently be completing their PhDs or have received their PhD after 1 May 2025; the doctoral degree must be completed prior to starting the Fellowship. Fellows may have the opportunity to teach undergraduates during their Fellowship. Apply via the Harvard Careers posting linked from the Rowland Institute fellowships page. Applications are currently open; the call does not state a fixed closing date, so check the Harvard Careers posting for the current deadline.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: NEH: Public Humanities Projects (2026-27 deadlines)
item.description:
Organisation: National Endowment for the Humanities (NEH)

Location: United States (applicants must be eligible US non-profits - museums, libraries, historical organisations, or accredited US colleges and universities)

Award: Planning grants up to USD 75,000; Implementation grants up to USD 1,000,000 (most awards substantially smaller). Two upcoming deadlines: 15 October 2026 and 9 December 2026.

Duration: Project-based

Deadline: 15 Oct 2026

NEH project grants supporting interpretive exhibitions, historic-place programming and discussion programmes that bring humanities scholarship to public audiences. Aimed at strengthening the humanities in public life through scholarship-informed public engagement (rather than primary research). Two upcoming application windows: 15 October 2026 and 9 December 2026. Two scales: Planning grants up to USD 75,000, and Implementation grants up to USD 1,000,000 (most awards substantially smaller than the cap). ELIGIBILITY: US-incorporated non-profits with appropriate IRS status, libraries, museums, historical organisations, or accredited US colleges and universities; individual scholars participate as project directors on behalf of an eligible institution. Aimed at projects that engage broad public audiences with humanities content (history, culture, philosophy, literature, etc.).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Future of Work Reporting Fellowship 2026 to 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Work Shift, in partnership with New America's Future of Work and Innovation Economy initiative

Location: United States (US-based journalists)

Award: $5,000 stipend + editorial coaching + access to expert sources + story amplification

Duration: 1 year (September 2026 to August 2027)

Deadline: 24 Jul 2026

One-year reporting fellowship supporting early- and mid-career US-based journalists to produce in-depth, place-based reporting on how education, workforce development and emerging technologies are reshaping economic opportunities across the United States. Open to print, digital, radio, television, multimedia and freelance journalists. Fellows receive a $5,000 stipend, editorial coaching, expert-source access and amplification of their stories.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: The Bennett Prize 2027 (Round 5)
item.description:
Organisation: The Bennett Prize / Muskegon Museum of Art

Location: Muskegon, Michigan, US (national reach)

Award: $75,000 ($37,500/year over 2 years) + traveling solo exhibition; additional $10,000 for one finalist

Duration: 2 year award period

Deadline: 19 Sep 2026

$75,000 prize for women figurative realist painters, awarded by a five-member jury. The winner receives $37,500 each year for two years to create a solo exhibition that travels nationally; one finalist additionally receives $10,000. Open to emerging artists who have not yet achieved full professional recognition.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Film Independent: Screenwriting Lab 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Film Independent

Location: Los Angeles, US

Award: Lab fellowship + access to bundled $10K fellowships (Cayton-Goldrich, MPAC, Sony Music Vision) and Climate Entertainment Development Grant ($25K for climate-focused fiction features)

Duration: Lab cohort

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

Competitive screenwriting lab for emerging feature screenwriters. Selected fellows are eligible for the Climate Entertainment Development Grant ($25K, climate-focused fiction features) and the same bundled $10K fellowship pool available across Artist Development programs (Cayton-Goldrich, MPAC Hollywood Bureau, Sony Music Vision). International fellows are also eligible for the Dolby Institute Fellowship ($50K post-production grant utilising Dolby Vision and Atmos). Lab alums become eligible to apply to the Amplifier Fellowship for Black filmmakers ($30K unrestricted plus year-long support; six fellows annually). Applications open 29 June 2026; non-member deadline 31 August 2026; Film Independent member extension to 14 September 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Film Independent: Episodic Directing Intensive 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Film Independent

Location: Los Angeles, US

Award: Lab fellowship + access to bundled $10K fellowships (Cayton-Goldrich, MPAC, Sony Music Vision) for selected fellows

Duration: Intensive cohort

Deadline: 28 Sep 2026

Intensive program for emerging episodic (TV/series) directors, with mentorship, set shadowing opportunities and industry access. Selected fellows are eligible for the same bundled $10K fellowship pool (Cayton-Goldrich, MPAC Hollywood Bureau, Sony Music Vision) available across Artist Development programs. Applications open 27 July 2026; non-member deadline 28 September 2026; Film Independent member extension to 12 October 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Fund for Investigative Journalism (FIJ) Grants
item.description:
Organisation: Fund for Investigative Journalism

Location: USA-primary (foreign-based stories require strong U.S. angle)

Award: Up to $10,000 (regular) or $1,000 to $2,500 (seed)

Duration: Story-based

Deadline: 14 Sep 2026

Grants for in-depth investigative reporting that exposes corruption, malfeasance or misuse of power across public and private sectors. Covers print, online, broadcast, books, documentaries and podcasts. Surveillance, abuse-of-power and accountability investigations all fit. Letter of Commitment from a news outlet required for full proposals (not for seed). Seed deadline ~10 May 2026; regular deadline 14 September 2026, 23:59 ET. Reviewed three to four times per year. Stories must be published in English with a U.S. media outlet. Ethnic media and journalists of colour particularly encouraged.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: MacDowell Fellowship
item.description:
Organisation: MacDowell

Location: Peterborough, New Hampshire, USA

Award: No residency fee; need-based stipends and travel reimbursement available; ~300 fellowships/year

Duration: Up to 8 weeks (Spring/Summer 2027 cycle: 1 March to 31 August 2027)

Deadline: 10 Sep 2026

Residency for artists across seven disciplines (architecture, film/video, interdisciplinary, literature, music composition, theatre, visual arts). Sole selection criterion is artistic excellence. Applications open 15 August 2026. February deadline of the following year covers the Fall/Winter cycle.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).
